chartPlan.title: India sends the world's largest workforce out to work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Any account of how India works has to start with the sheer size of the labour pool it has to absorb.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line chart of total labour force (people) from 1990 to today; look at the upward trajectory and the latest 61.8 crore figure.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse this with PLFS numbers; it is a World Bank modelled estimate.

chartPlan.title: A young country, in its working years
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's labour story rides on its demography: the working-age share is near its peak, the 'dividend' window.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line chart with three lines (0-14, 15-64, 65+); note the rising working-age share and falling child share.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the dividend is automatic; it requires job creation.

chartPlan.title: Who shows up: participation and employment
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Two of the three headline numbers: how many Indians offer to work, and how many are working.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: LFPR (green) and WPR (blue); the small gap is the unemployed. Notice the rise from 2017-18 to 2023-24.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret LFPR as employment rate; it includes unemployed who are looking.

chartPlan.title: The unemployment rate is low, and that is the puzzle
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's headline joblessness is strikingly low for a poor country, which is itself the central paradox of how India works.
chartPlan.howToRead: Single line falling from 6% to 3.2%; the downward trend masks distress-driven employment.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not take low UR as a sign of healthy labour market; it often means people take any work available.

chartPlan.title: India works less than its neighbours
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Put India next to its peers and it is a participation outlier, fewer of its adults are in the workforce at all.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line chart with India at the bottom (55.7%), Vietnam on top (72.8%); observe the gap.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Remember modelled ILO rates differ from PLFS; comparisons are valid across countries using the same source.

chartPlan.title: The single biggest reason India works less: women
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The gap between male and female participation is the largest single fact about who works in India.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: male LFPR high and flat around 78%, female LFPR low but rising from 23% to 42%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not miss that female LFPR in PLFS (usual status) is higher than World Bank estimates.

chartPlan.title: Where the women returning to work are
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The female rebound is overwhelmingly a rural, farm-based story, not an office one.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: rural female LFPR (solid) rising strongly, urban (dashed) barely moving; gap widens.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume rising female LFPR means more formal jobs; it is mainly own-account agricultural work.

chartPlan.title: India's women work less than almost anywhere comparable
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Even after the recent rise, India sits near the bottom of the world on women in the workforce.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: India lowest (32.4%), Vietnam highest (68.6%); note the 2023-24 PLFS figure is higher than this modelled estimate.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Use this for relative position, not absolute female LFPR level; PLFS gives higher numbers.

chartPlan.title: Four in ten young women are doing nothing officially counted
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The missing-women story has a sharp young edge: a huge share of young women are neither working, in school, nor in training, the NEET measure.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines: female NEET highest (39.7%), male lowest (12.2%), total in between; declining trend but still high.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: NEET includes those outside labour force; it is not the same as unemployment rate.

chartPlan.title: Most Indians don't have a job, they have work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The defining feature of how India works: the typical worker runs a tiny own-account enterprise, not holds a salaried job.
chartPlan.howToRead: Stacked area or multi-line: self-employed (58.4%), casual (19.8%), regular wage (21.7%). Note self-employment rising, regular wage stagnant.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not equate self-employed with entrepreneur; most are own-account workers with no employees.

chartPlan.title: Secure formal jobs stay scarce
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The long World Bank view confirms the survey: wage employment grows only slowly while vulnerable work stays high.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines: self-employed declining slowly, wage/salaried rising slowly, vulnerable employment still above 70%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Vulnerable employment is a subset of self-employment (own-account + unpaid family workers) and does not include casual labour.

chartPlan.title: How informal India's work really is
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The ILO's official informality measure puts a hard number on the informality engine: the overwhelming majority of Indian jobs have no contract, no social security, no protection.
chartPlan.howToRead: Four lines: total 87.2%, agriculture 98.6%, industry 83.4%, services 75.8%; agriculture nearly 100% informal.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Informal employment is defined by the job, not the sector; even formal sector enterprises can hire informal workers.

chartPlan.title: Informal work, India versus its peers
chartPlan.whyShowThis: High informality is common across developing Asia, but India sits at the top of the range.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: India highest (87.2%), Vietnam lowest (67.0%); all lines show gradual decline.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Data coverage differs; Vietnam's 2024 figure vs Indonesia's 2023.

chartPlan.title: Formal hiring, month by month
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The clearest high-frequency signal of formal-sector job creation is who is newly paying into the pension fund.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line chart of net new EPF subscribers per month; observe peaks and seasonality; latest 2.1 million.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Net new includes re-registrations and may double-count multiple jobs; it is not net employment.

chartPlan.title: India's workers are still on the farm
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The textbook path runs farm to factory to office. India's workers largely stopped at the first step, and lately drifted back.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: agriculture 43.5%, industry 24.9%, services 31.6%; agriculture share increased from 2017-18.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Current weekly status may show higher agricultural share due to seasonality.

chartPlan.title: ...but the economy's output moved to services long ago
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Set output beside employment and the scissors open: services dominate what India produces while farms dominate who works.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: agriculture 16.3%, industry 24.6%, services 49.9%; long-term decline in agriculture share of output.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are GVA shares (before taxes/subsidies); not exactly GDP shares.

chartPlan.title: Stuck on the land, compared with peers
chartPlan.whyShowThis: China and Vietnam pulled workers off farms far faster than India; the comparison shows the shift is possible.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: India 41.6%, Bangladesh 44.3%, Vietnam 25.0%, China 21.7%; note Vietnam's steep decline.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: World Bank modelled; India's PLFS figure (43.5%) is close.

chartPlan.title: The very long view: India's income over the centuries
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Today's labour market is one frame in a much longer film, India was a large share of the world economy, then fell far behind, and is now climbing back.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line chart of GDP per capita (Maddison) in 2011 int‑$ from 1 CE to 2022; flat for centuries then take-off after 1950.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Early historical data is reconstructed; focus on broad trends.

chartPlan.title: What a month of work earns, and the gulf between kinds of work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Pay is where informality bites: the salaried earn far more per month than the self-employed majority.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: regular wage ₹20,702, self-employed ₹13,279; gap persists and may widen.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Nominal ₹; self-employed includes expenses, so net income lower.

chartPlan.title: The salary freeze: pay rose on paper, not in real life
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single most important thing about Indian pay this decade: once you strip out inflation, salaried earnings have not risen, they have fallen.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: nominal wages rising, real wages (2012 ₹) falling from ₹12,101 to ₹11,112.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Real wages are deflated by CPI-IW; different deflators give slightly different trends.

chartPlan.title: The casual daily wage
chartPlan.whyShowThis: At the bottom of the ladder, work is paid by the day, and that daily rate sets the floor for hundreds of millions.
chartPlan.howToRead: Single line: casual daily wage rising from ₹256 to ₹418 over 7 years.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Again, nominal; real increase is smaller.

chartPlan.title: Rural wages since the 1990s
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Rural wages set the living standard for most Indian workers and reveal a persistent gender gap.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: men ₹454, women ₹322; men's wages always higher, gap in nominal terms.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are nominal blended series; the gender gap in real terms may differ.

chartPlan.title: Rural wages, once you strip out inflation
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The same deflation applied to rural wages shows how much of the headline rise is real gain versus just keeping up with prices.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines of real wages (2012 ₹): men ₹232, women ₹164.7; modest rise over the years, especially for men.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: CPI-Rural deflator may not match actual consumption baskets of rural workers.

chartPlan.title: How much each worker produces, and how far behind
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Wages can only rise sustainably if output per worker rises; this is where the farm-overhang shows up as a number.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: India $24,430, China $45,842, Vietnam $26,091; India's productivity increased but remains below peers.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Productivity is output per employed person, not per hour; hours worked differ across countries.

chartPlan.title: Having a job is not the same as escaping poverty
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Low unemployment hides a different problem: many Indians who do work are still poor. Working poverty is the missing half of the jobs story.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: all workers 3.6%, youth 8.3%; dramatic decline from 44% in 2000.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Poverty line is $3.65/day PPP 2017; higher than India's national line.

chartPlan.title: The paradox: the more educated you are, the more likely you are jobless
chartPlan.whyShowThis: This single chart explains why low headline unemployment and a real jobs crisis are both true at once.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bar chart: not literate 0.2%, graduate 13%, postgraduate 12.4%; upward slope.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Causality is not proven; higher education may correlate with ability to wait for better jobs.

chartPlan.title: It is the young who can't find work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The overall jobless rate hides the crisis: among the young entering the labour market, unemployment is several times higher.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: youth (15-29) 10.2%, overall 3.2%; both declined but gap remains.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Youth defined as 15-29 in PLFS; World Bank uses 15-24.

chartPlan.title: Young and jobless, in context
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Youth joblessness is high across South and Southeast Asia, but India's is severe given how many young people it has.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multi-line: India 16.0%, World 13.4%; Vietnam 6.2% is lowest.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: World Bank uses 15-24; PLFS youth are 15-29, so numbers differ.

chartPlan.title: Unemployment by social group
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Who is unemployed also tracks social position, in ways that complicate the simple education story.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bar chart: Others 3.8%, SC 3.3%, OBC 3.1%, ST 1.9%; not a simple gradient.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Education level overlap with social group may explain part of the pattern.

chartPlan.title: Unemployment, now month by month
chartPlan.whyShowThis: From 2025 India finally publishes monthly labour data, turning an annual snapshot into something closer to a live feed.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line chart: monthly UR fluctuating around 5-6% range; latest 5.2% (April 2026).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Monthly series uses CWS, so rates are higher than usual status annual figures.

chartPlan.title: When private work runs out, India falls back on a guarantee
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The rural job-guarantee scheme is the shock absorber of India's labour market: demand for it spikes exactly when other work disappears.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line chart of persons demanding work per month; seasonal spikes; latest 27.4 million.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Administrative data; demand may exceed actual work provided.

chartPlan.title: The rural safety valve, in days of work
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Behind the demand figure sits the actual work delivered, days of employment created when nothing else is available.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: person-days created (109.9 million in April 2026) and active workers (133.3 million).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Person-days include multiple days per worker; not distinct workers.

chartPlan.title: What the hiring market is signalling next
chartPlan.whyShowThis: To close, a forward-looking read: the private hiring market for the formal, white-collar jobs that India's graduates are waiting for.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line chart of JobSpeak index; latest 2836; look for trend direction and recent movements.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Index is based on online job postings on Naukri.com; not representative of all hiring.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, because they cover the full story: scale, demographics, participation, unemployment, gender, informality, sectoral structure, wages, productivity, education, youth, social groups, safety net, and a forward-looking hiring index.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Phone reader needs a clean long-term trend without clutter; annual data from 1990 shows growth and size.
Three age-group lines show the dividend window; annual view is enough to see the demographic transition.
Two lines (LFPR, WPR) on the same chart highlight the gap; annual PLFS periodicity matches survey releases.
The education gradient is best shown with the latest survey year's bar chart; a line chart would overwhelm.
Four sector lines reveal informality structure; annual fits ILO's SDG reporting.
Monthly data captures seasonal distress spikes; a line chart with sparse ticks works on phone.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The percentage of the working‑age population (15+) that is either employed or actively looking for work. Think of it as 'how many adults are in the game'.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: LFPR is the master dial of how India works; a low LFPR means a huge share of working‑age people are neither working nor seeking work.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The share of the working‑age population that is actually employed. It is similar to LFPR but excludes those who are looking without success.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: WPR tells you the real absorption of workers, and the gap between LFPR and WPR is the unemployment pool.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The share of the labour force (not the whole population) that wants work but cannot find it. A rising UR can mean a jobs crisis, but a low UR in a poor country often means people cannot afford to stay idle.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's low headline UR (3.2%) hides the fact that many ‘employed’ persons are in low‑quality, subsistence work.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Own‑account workers plus contributing family workers, people with no employment security, no regular income, and no social protection. It is the ILO's measure of precarious self‑employment.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Over 70% of Indian workers are in vulnerable employment, explaining why a ‘job’ often does not mean a payslip.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The share of employed persons whose jobs are not covered by social insurance, not registered, and not subject to labour law. It is the ILO’s broad measure of informality, covering both the unorganised sector and informal workers in the organised sector.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: At 87%, it quantifies the scale of India’s informal economy, far higher than peers like Vietnam.

glossaryPlan.term: NEET (Not in Employment, Education, or Training)
glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The percentage of youth (15–24) who are neither working, studying, nor undergoing any vocational training. It captures disconnection from both the labour market and skill‑building.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: A 39.7% female NEET rate exposes the depth of women’s exclusion from meaningful activity.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The historical shift of workers out of low‑productivity agriculture into higher‑productivity industry and services as an economy develops. Successful transformation raises average incomes because workers produce more.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India’s workers are stuck on the farm (43.5%) while output is in services (49.9% of GDP), creating a productivity trap.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, a law that guarantees 100 days of wage‑employment per year to any rural household willing to do unskilled manual work. It acts as a floor for rural wages and a safety net when other work dries up.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Spikes in MGNREGA demand signal distress in the rural private labour market; it is the ‘canary in the coal mine’ for rural employment.

proseRules: No AI tells (examine, pattern, important, moreover, etc.)
Use only lockedNumbers, sourceSummaries, and evidence packet content
No outside facts, rankings, or policy claims
Question‑led H2 sections matching the chart order
Explain every economic concept in plain Indian English
Use displayValue from lockedNumbers when available
No rough calculations unless derived numbers present in evidence
Keep each H2 section 120‑200 words, genuinely answering its heading

evidence.question: How does India work?

theme.label: Society, work, education, and services
readerQuestions: What basic capability is being measured?
How many people does the percentage represent?
Is this access, usage, quality, or outcome?
Which caveat stops the chart from being overclaimed?

plannedCharts.title: India sends the world's largest workforce out to work
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · total labour force · 1990 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Any account of how India works has to start with the sheer size of the labour pool it has to absorb.

plannedCharts.title: A young country, in its working years
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · share of population by age · 1960 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's labour story rides on its demography: the working-age share is near its peak, the 'dividend' window.

plannedCharts.title: Who shows up: participation and employment
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · usual status (PS+SS), 15+ · survey years 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Two of the three headline numbers: how many Indians offer to work, and how many are working.

plannedCharts.title: The unemployment rate is low, and that is the puzzle
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · unemployment rate, usual status, 15+
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's headline joblessness is strikingly low for a poor country, which is itself the central paradox of how India works.

plannedCharts.title: India works less than its neighbours
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank (modelled ILO) · labour force participation · 1990 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Put India next to its peers and it is a participation outlier, fewer of its adults are in the workforce at all.

plannedCharts.title: The single biggest reason India works less: women
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · LFPR by sex, 15+ · 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The gap between male and female participation is the largest single fact about who works in India.

plannedCharts.title: Where the women returning to work are
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · female LFPR by sector, 15+
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The female rebound is overwhelmingly a rural, farm-based story, not an office one.

plannedCharts.title: India's women work less than almost anywhere comparable
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank (modelled ILO) · female labour force participation · 1990 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Even after the recent rise, India sits near the bottom of the world on women in the workforce.

plannedCharts.title: Four in ten young women are doing nothing officially counted
plannedCharts.subtitle: ILOSTAT (SDG 8.6.1) · youth not in employment, education or training
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The missing-women story has a sharp young edge: a huge share of young women are neither working, in school, nor in training, the NEET measure.

plannedCharts.title: Most Indians don't have a job, they have work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · distribution of workers by status · 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The defining feature of how India works: the typical worker runs a tiny own-account enterprise, not holds a salaried job.

plannedCharts.title: Secure formal jobs stay scarce
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank (modelled ILO) · status in employment, India · 1991 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The long World Bank view confirms the survey: wage employment grows only slowly while vulnerable work stays high.

plannedCharts.title: How informal India's work really is
plannedCharts.subtitle: ILOSTAT (SDG 8.3.1) · informal employment rate · 2010 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The ILO's official informality measure puts a hard number on the informality engine: the overwhelming majority of Indian jobs have no contract, no social security, no protection.

plannedCharts.title: Informal work, India versus its peers
plannedCharts.subtitle: ILOSTAT (SDG 8.3.1) · informal employment rate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: High informality is common across developing Asia, but India sits at the top of the range.

plannedCharts.title: Formal hiring, month by month
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · net new EPF subscribers · monthly
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The clearest high-frequency signal of formal-sector job creation is who is newly paying into the pension fund.

plannedCharts.title: India's workers are still on the farm
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · workers by broad sector (current weekly status) · 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The textbook path runs farm to factory to office. India's workers largely stopped at the first step, and lately drifted back.

plannedCharts.title: ...but the economy's output moved to services long ago
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · gross value added by sector · share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Set output beside employment and the scissors open: services dominate what India produces while farms dominate who works.

plannedCharts.title: Stuck on the land, compared with peers
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank (modelled ILO) · employment in agriculture · 1991 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: China and Vietnam pulled workers off farms far faster than India; the comparison shows the shift is possible.

plannedCharts.title: The very long view: India's income over the centuries
plannedCharts.subtitle: Our World in Data · Maddison Project · real GDP per capita
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's labour market is one frame in a much longer film, India was a large share of the world economy, then fell far behind, and is now climbing back.

plannedCharts.title: What a month of work earns, and the gulf between kinds of work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · average monthly earnings · 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Pay is where informality bites: the salaried earn far more per month than the self-employed majority.

plannedCharts.title: The salary freeze: pay rose on paper, not in real life
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS earnings · nominal vs CPI-deflated (2012 ₹) · 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single most important thing about Indian pay this decade: once you strip out inflation, salaried earnings have not risen, they have fallen.

plannedCharts.title: The casual daily wage
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · average daily earnings, casual labour · ₹ per day
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: At the bottom of the ladder, work is paid by the day, and that daily rate sets the floor for hundreds of millions.

plannedCharts.title: Rural wages since the 1990s
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · daily average rural wage rate (blended series)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Rural wages set the living standard for most Indian workers and reveal a persistent gender gap.

plannedCharts.title: Rural wages, once you strip out inflation
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub rural wage deflated by MOSPI CPI-Rural (2012=100)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same deflation applied to rural wages shows how much of the headline rise is real gain versus just keeping up with prices.

plannedCharts.title: How much each worker produces, and how far behind
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · GDP per person employed · 1991 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Wages can only rise sustainably if output per worker rises; this is where the farm-overhang shows up as a number.

plannedCharts.title: Having a job is not the same as escaping poverty
plannedCharts.subtitle: ILOSTAT (SDG 1.1.1) · employed living below US$3.65/day PPP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Low unemployment hides a different problem: many Indians who do work are still poor. Working poverty is the missing half of the jobs story.

plannedCharts.title: The paradox: the more educated you are, the more likely you are jobless
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · unemployment rate by education, 15+ · latest survey year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This single chart explains why low headline unemployment and a real jobs crisis are both true at once.

plannedCharts.title: It is the young who can't find work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · unemployment rate, all ages vs 15-29 · 2017-18 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The overall jobless rate hides the crisis: among the young entering the labour market, unemployment is several times higher.

plannedCharts.title: Young and jobless, in context
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank (modelled ILO) · youth unemployment (15-24) · 1991 to today
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Youth joblessness is high across South and Southeast Asia, but India's is severe given how many young people it has.

plannedCharts.title: Unemployment by social group
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS · unemployment rate by social category, 15+ · latest survey year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Who is unemployed also tracks social position, in ways that complicate the simple education story.

plannedCharts.title: Unemployment, now month by month
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · monthly PLFS unemployment rate · 2025 onward
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: From 2025 India finally publishes monthly labour data, turning an annual snapshot into something closer to a live feed.

plannedCharts.title: When private work runs out, India falls back on a guarantee
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · persons demanding work under MGNREGA · monthly
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The rural job-guarantee scheme is the shock absorber of India's labour market: demand for it spikes exactly when other work disappears.

plannedCharts.title: The rural safety valve, in days of work
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · MGNREGA · monthly
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Behind the demand figure sits the actual work delivered, days of employment created when nothing else is available.

plannedCharts.title: What the hiring market is signalling next
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · Naukri JobSpeak white-collar hiring index · monthly
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: To close, a forward-looking read: the private hiring market for the formal, white-collar jobs that India's graduates are waiting for.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
